next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

By late June the shuttle to Zurich left twice a week. On the Tuesday the relay group went, Kai came in off the indoor track with his shirt stuck to his back and stopped by the front windows while Ursula stacked breakfast cups behind him. A Meridian coordinator in a navy jacket checked names against a tablet. Four relay runners climbed aboard with matching duffel bags and the same new corporate luggage tag looped through each handle. Then the Czech sprinter went, then the two South Korean gymnasts. The shuttle door folded shut. Somebody laughed on the other side of the glass. Then it pulled down the switchback road and was gone.

He cut through the residential wing on his way to the showers. Half the room displays were dark now. The ones still on floated pulse traces and battery diagnostics over empty beds. Most of the institute had made its bargain. By then the holdouts were a handful spread between Kirchberg and Davos: Kai still running nothing, Lena and two others on Yusuf's patch, everyone else signed or already gone. Choices had stopped sounding like arguments. They were just departures.

That afternoon he found Margot at the bottle filler by the side entrance, bent over from the gravel loop below the treeline with one hand braced on the wall. Sweat had soaked the back of her grey sweatshirt. Margot drank until the sensor cut off, hit it again, drank more. When she straightened, her hand went to the pale strap mark the feed interface had left on her wrist. Her thumbnail dug into it hard enough to blanch the skin.

"You all right?" he asked.

"Not especiall...

payoffs_to_land.description: Margot's feed sessions exposed athletes to additional virus propagation risk—the transmission vector she identified in Chapter 6 and concealed

prev_tail_excerpt:
...d told you about the archive, you'd have reported me. The data would've been locked down and Farah —"

"Don't." His wrist ached. His shoulder ached. "Don't make this about Farah right now. A kid is paralyzed and you've been sitting on this." He gestured at the monitors. "All of it."

"I didn't have enough to —"

"Enough for what? For who?" Kai looked at him. "Tomas doesn't need your standard of evidence, Yusuf. He just needs to know what happened to him."

Yusuf did not answer. He kept looking at the monitors, at the yellow blocks lined up beside each other, as if staring hard enough might make them settle into proof. His hands were on the desk. They were shaking.

"What do you want to do?" Yusuf asked.

Kai looked through the glass wall at the calibration bay beyond, the rigs where athletes lay down and trusted the system to maintain them, the fluorescent lights, the humming processors. Three corridors away, Tomas was on an imaging table learning the dimensions of his new life. One floor above, Margot was sitting with the phantom of his paralysis still alive in her legs. Outside, the spring thaw was pulling the last of the winter off the mountains and the meltwater kept running downhill.

"I don't know," Kai said.

He left the office with the sliding door stuck half-open behind him. Yusuf's monitor light fell across the calibration bay floor in yellow bars. The rigs sat under the fluorescents with their straps loose, ready for the next athlete to lie down and hand the room his body. A few weeks ago Kai would have done it without thinking. Now the whole place looked rotten.
state_deltas_required: The full cost of concealment, addiction, and institutional failure becomes legible
Margot's addiction fully exposed; the Meridian conspiracy connects but remains unprovable; the crisis cost is permanent
